title: Spinning llama fibre
subtitle: Preparing and spinning the light, scale-free fibre of the llama
excerpt: Llama fibre has no scales on the individual fibres, which makes it slippery to spin but produces a lightweight, non-felting yarn with a soft drape. This entry covers fibre selection, preparation, and spinning a consistent singles on the wheel.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from general camelid fibre spinning references. Llama fibre properties described in Patricia Foreman's Llamas Are the Ultimate (2002, in copyright, substance only referenced) and in breed fibre guides from the British Llama Society.

glossaryTerms.definition: The diameter of an individual fibre in micrometres. The lower the number, the finer the fibre. Llama typically runs between 20 and 40 microns depending on the animal and the part of the fleece.

A spinning method in which fibres are drafted from a carded preparation with the hands held further apart, allowing more air into the yarn and producing a lofty, elastic result. Suitable for llama because it helps compensate for the fibre's natural lack of spring.
A yarn built from two singles twisted together in the opposite direction to the singles twist. Two-ply adds stability; single-ply llama tends to bias when knitted or woven.

content.text: Llama fibre is structurally different from wool: the individual fibres have no scales, which means they do not felt but also do not grip each other during drafting. This requires a slightly higher twist during spinning than a comparable wool, and it rewards a

that adds a little air and spring to compensate for the fibre's natural drape. A llama-wool blend (70:30 or 80:20) is easier to handle for a first llama project; pure llama requires more wheel control.

attrs.title: Wheel safety
attrs.body: Keep loose hair and clothing clear of the drive band and flyer. Tie hair back before sitting at the wheel.

items.name: Combed llama top or washed llama locks

content.text: The double coat of the llama consists of a fine inner fibre (the down) and coarser outer guard hairs. The down is the desirable spinning fibre; guard hairs should be removed by hand before spinning. Commercially prepared llama top has already been dehaired. If working from raw locks, sort each lock gently by pulling the coarser guard hairs free from the softer inner fibres.

of the batch if it is listed by the supplier; fibre above 30 microns will feel noticeably coarser next to the skin than finer grades. Card gently with hand cards if working from washed locks; avoid drum carding llama as it produces a flat, compressed batt with little loft.

Set the wheel to a moderate ratio (around 8:1 to 10:1) with firm uptake tension to compensate for the slippery fibre. Spin with a woollen draw, keeping the twist entering the drafting zone more freely than you would with wool. Llama needs more twist per inch than wool to hold together; under-twisted singles will pull apart at the slightest tension. The yarn should feel springy to a gentle tug when you stop the wheel, not limp.
Ply two singles together from the lazy kate using the

method in the opposite direction to the singles. Llama gains significantly in stability and drape when plied; single-ply llama yarns bias badly in finished fabric. Set the twist in lukewarm water with a gentle soak of 15 minutes. Dry flat; llama has no crimp to return it to shape under tension.

items.problem: The singles keep drafting apart between the hands
items.cause: Not enough twist is entering the drafting zone, or the fibre is not sufficiently prepared
items.fix: Slow the backward drafting hand and allow more twist to enter the zone before drawing back. If using raw locks, card more thoroughly to open up the fibres before spinning.

items.problem: The finished yarn feels scratchy despite selecting fine-grade fibre
items.cause: Guard hairs were not fully removed, or the fibre is above 28 microns
items.fix: Sort the locks more carefully, running each one between two fingers to check for coarser guard hairs before carding. Blend the llama 70:30 with fine merino to soften the handle.
